text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the diseased left colon laterally, using a lateral to medial approach. The mobilization progresses towards the splenic flexure, with careful handling to avoid excessive retraction on the spleen and prevent splenic capsular tears.

The surgeon begins mobilization of the distal transverse colon, dissecting the greater omentum to access the lesser sac. The dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure to connect with the previously mobilized left colon.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the left colon from the retroperitoneum. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is dissected and skeletonized, then ligated using an endoscopic stapler. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is similarly dissected and ligated, with care taken to avoid the left ureter. The middle colic vessels are dissected and ligated with an endoscopic stapler, utilizing a 12mm port in the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon mobilizes the right colon from a supracolic approach, advancing the dissection of the proximal transverse colon towards the hepatic flexure. The duodenum and head of the pancreas are clearly visible, with careful attention to avoid injury to these structures during the inferior dissection.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the right colon using a lateral to medial infracolic approach. The ileocolic pedicle is dissected, and an endoscopic stapler is used to ligate the ileocolic vessels.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the rectum by opening the peritoneal reflection on the right lateral aspect of the upper rectum, followed by the left lateral aspect. The rectum is then mobilized circumferentially in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection of the rectum, taking care to avoid injury to the vagina. The dissection proceeds circumferentially around the rectum, with the posterior rectal dissection advancing towards the pelvic floor.

The surgeon uses gentle pushing of the tissue to reveal the tissue plane before cutting during the dissection. After completing the intra-abdominal dissection of the rectum, the perineal dissection begins via the intersphincteric plane to enter the pelvic cavity and deliver the specimen. Diseased perianal skin is excised, and the terminal ileum is divided with a linear stapler. The levators are closed with absorbable suture, followed by additional layers of absorbable suture applied to the fat layer and skin.

The surgeon re-establishes pneumoperitoneum to verify the correct orientation of the mesentery before maturing the stoma. An inspection confirms hemostasis, and a nineteen Blake drain is placed in the pelvis. After creating the ileostomy, a final inspection ensures the correct orientation, highlighting a straight line through the cut edge of the small bowel mesentery down to the SMA origin.
